topic: Two colleagues are both considered for the same promotion
raw_output:
[story 1]

The fluorescent lights of the office hummed with an unnerving intensity, casting a stark glare on the documents spread across my desk. I kept re-reading the job description, my eyes skipping over the bullet points as if searching for an escape route. Sarah, across the aisle, was laughing easily with Mark, a light, breezy sound that grated on my nerves. Every time the manager’s voice echoed through the open-plan space, a cold tightness began to coil in my stomach. I gripped my pen so hard my knuckles turned white, focusing intently on the minuscule scratch marks on the desk surface, trying to anchor myself to something solid. I knew I had done my work well, but the sheer weight of the competition felt crushing, a physical pressure building behind my eyes.

[story 2]

"Well, that's a wrap," the supervisor announced, clapping his hands together with an air of finality. My breath hitched, a shallow, involuntary intake of air. I watched as Sarah smoothly collected her portfolio, her smile radiating an effortless confidence that felt like a direct challenge. I felt a sudden, strange buzzing sensation behind my temples, a frantic energy that made my hands tremble slightly as I tried to gather my scattered notes. *This isn't fair,* I thought, the thought sharp and immediate. I stood up too quickly, the chair scraping loudly against the floor, and walked toward the water cooler, needing the cold shock of the metal to steady the sudden internal tremor.

[story 3]

I stared at the framed commendations on the wall, the gold lettering seeming to mock my own recent achievements. The air in the conference room felt thick, suffocating. When the decision was finally announced, the silence that followed was deafening. My mouth went dry, and I could feel a distinct heat rising up my neck. I forced a polite nod, my voice sounding unnaturally high as I managed, "Congratulations to both of you." Internally, a frantic scramble began—a rapid-fire review of every interaction, every missed opportunity, every perceived failing. My gaze darted between Sarah and Mark, searching for any sign of weakness, anything to justify the churning anxiety tightening its grip around my chest.

I kept re-reading the job description, my eyes skipping over the bullet points as if searching for an escape route. Sarah, across the aisle, was laughing easily with Mark, a light, breezy sound that grated on my nerves. I watched as Sarah smoothly collected her portfolio, her smile radiating an effortless confidence that felt like a direct challenge.
